draft.introduction: Milarepa is Tibet’s most beloved poet-saint. His songs express the direct experience of realization gained through years of extreme ascetic practice in Himalayan caves.

translation.text: In the hermitage of my body, the temple of the mind, the horse of my breath carries the rider of awareness. When the horse is still, the rider sees clearly. In the cave of emptiness, there is no fear. The snow mountain is my pillow; the stars are my blanket.
translation.original_text: lus kyi ri khrod sems kyi lha khang du | rlung rta rig pa’i zhon pa bzhon

translation.text: Those who meditate on the nature of mind need no other practice. The nature of mind is clear light—luminous, empty, unceasing. If you recognize it, you are a buddha. If you do not, you wander in samsara. But samsara and nirvana have never been apart.
translation.original_text: sems nyid ’od gsal stong pa ’gag med de | ngo shes na sangs rgyas

translator_notes.note: Milarepa’s use of the body as “hermitage” reverses the ascetic logic that led him to caves. The outer cave is a condition for discovering the inner cave—the body as a temple of awareness. This is tantric Buddhism’s key insight: the body is not an obstacle but the vehicle of realization.
